categoryDescription: Evaluates the ETF's historical returns, consistency, momentum, and (where relevant) the income / total-return split, daily-leverage fidelity, technical trend position, mandate alignment, and the trajectory of distributions. Maximum-drawdown severity, capture ratios, risk-adjusted-return quality, and rate-environment resilience are covered in Risk Analysis — this category stays focused on the return story itself. Benchmark and category comparison is no longer a standalone factor: it is folded into every return factor via group-specific instructions, since each ETF group has its own natural benchmark (broad equity → S&P 500 / MSCI EAFE / MSCI ACWI; sector / thematic → sector or theme index plus S&P 500; leveraged → unleveraged underlying × leverage factor; commodities / digital assets → spot benchmark; allocation → 60/40 blend; fixed income → duration-matched bond or credit index). Factors are assigned to one or more ETF groups defined in etf-analysis-categories.json; each group typically uses 4–5 factors from this file. Each factor carries a `groupInstructions` map keyed by group — the LLM should treat the entry for the fund's group as the authoritative comparison/perspective rule on top of the generic `factorDescription`.

factors.factorDescription: Measures compound annual growth rate over 5, 10, 15, and 20-year periods. Shows whether the fund has consistently grown investor capital over extended horizons. For funds younger than 5 years, note the limited history and use only the periods available rather than extrapolating. Every CAGR claim must carry the same-period number for the group-appropriate benchmark (see `groupInstructions`) so the retail reader can answer 'is this CAGR actually good?' rather than guess. A naked CAGR without a comparison point should not survive the draft.
factors.factorMetrics: cagr5y, cagr10y, cagr15y, cagr20y, returnsAnnual, returnsTrailing

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Compare CAGR to the broad index the fund tracks (S&P 500 for US large blend, Russell 1000 Growth / Value for US style tilts, MSCI EAFE for foreign developed, MSCI ACWI for global). Quote the basis-point gap across every available window (5/10/15/20Y) and label whether the fund matched, beat, or trailed.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Compare to both the sector / theme index the fund tracks (XL-series sector index, MSCI Information Tech, S&P Real Estate, etc.) AND the S&P 500 — retail's real question is whether the narrow bet beat just owning the broad market over a long horizon. For single-country EM, use the country index (MSCI China, Nifty 50, MSCI Brazil) plus MSCI Emerging Markets as the diversified-EM reference.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Long-horizon CAGR vs the unleveraged underlying is the decay test — quote the underlying's CAGR times the stated leverage factor as the textbook expectation, then show the realized result. Frame the gap explicitly as compounding decay; do not compare to the S&P 500 directly here.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Compare CAGR to the spot benchmark — LBMA Gold price, COMEX silver, Bloomberg Commodity Index for broad baskets, CME CF BTC / ETH Reference Rate for crypto. Also juxtapose the S&P 500's same-period CAGR — retail's honest question is whether this asset beat just holding stocks over the long run.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare CAGR to a same-period 60/40 blend (S&P 500 + Bloomberg US Aggregate) and to the allocation peer category median. For target-date funds, use the dated 'as-of' year vs the Vanguard Target Retirement series as the standard reference; for tactical / global allocation, the question is whether active calls beat a passive 60/40.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Compare CAGR to the Bloomberg US Aggregate (broad core) or a duration-matched Treasury index (ICE BofA 1-3Y / 7-10Y / 20+Y Treasury). For muni funds, compare to the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index and quote tax-equivalent CAGR at a stated bracket (~32% federal baseline). For TIPS, use a TIPS index and separate real vs nominal returns.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Compare CAGR to the appropriate credit benchmark — Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield, S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan, ICE BofA US Preferred Stock, JPM EMBI Global Diversified, ICE BofA US Convertible. Also quote a same-period 60/40 CAGR — retail's honest question is whether they were actually paid for taking real default and subordination risk.

factors.factorDescription: Evaluates recent performance over 1-month, 3-month, 6-month, YTD, and 1-year periods. Distinguishes short-term momentum (1M, 3M) from trailing performance (6M, 1Y). A strong 1Y return alongside weak recent months usually reflects a normal pullback within an uptrend rather than broad weakness, while broad-based weakness across every window points to a category-wide drawdown the reader should size against history. Every short-term number needs the same-period number for the group-appropriate benchmark (see `groupInstructions`) so the reader can tell whether the move is fund-specific or just the asset class moving.
factors.factorMetrics: return1m, return3m, return6m, returnYtd, return1y, returnsAnnual

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the same window for the index the fund tracks (S&P 500 for US large blend, MSCI EAFE for foreign, MSCI ACWI for global, Russell 1000 style index for tilts). Flag whether near-term weakness is fund-specific (lagging the index) or a broad-market move that hit every peer.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Compare to both the sector / theme index AND the S&P 500 over each window — retail wants to see whether the sector bet is currently outperforming the broad market or fading. For single-country EM, compare to the country index plus MSCI EM; flag any divergence where the sector index moved differently from the fund (signals tracking issues or tilt drift).
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Short-term return is the entire decision frame here. Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the stated daily-leverage multiple of the unleveraged underlying (a 3x fund should be roughly 3x the underlying's same-period move minus reset slippage). Flag any large gap as path-dependency loss; do not compare to the S&P 500.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Compare to the spot underlying return (LBMA Gold, BCOM, CME CF BTC / ETH Reference Rate) over each window. For futures-based commodity funds, show the spot vs fund gap and call out roll cost (contango / backwardation) explicitly. Also juxtapose the S&P 500 for the retail diversification angle.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y total return to the underlying equity index the strategy writes options on (S&P 500 for JEPI / SPYI, Nasdaq-100 for QYLD / XYLD / QQQI). Retail's question: did the income offset the missed upside in this window, or did the option overlay cap rallies without delivering the headline yield?
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to a same-period 60/40 blend (S&P 500 + Bloomberg US Aggregate) and to the allocation peer-category median. For target-date funds, also compare to the issuer's published peer group for the dated year; for tactical / global allocation, retail wants to see whether the active calls beat a passive blend in the near term.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the duration-matched Treasury or core-bond index. For muni, compare to a national muni index of similar duration. Flag whether near-term moves are rate-driven (parallel with peers) or fund-specific (active manager calls, tracking drift).
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the matching credit benchmark (Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield, S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan, ICE BofA US Preferred Stock, JPM EMBI Global Diversified). Flag whether short-term weakness is spread-widening that hit the whole sub-asset class or fund-specific underperformance.

factors.factorDescription: Assesses how stable returns have been across different periods and calendar years. Quote the calendar-year hit rate (how often the fund had a positive year over its available history), the size of its worst calendar year, and how percentile-rank trends have moved year-over-year. Cite the actual rank movement (e.g. `14 → 87 → 18`), not just 'volatile'. The benchmark-matched bad year rule applies: if the fund's worst year is roughly in line with both its stated index and its category in the same window, that is the asset class moving, not fund failure.
factors.factorMetrics: returnsAnnual, return1y, return3y, return5y, return10y, percentileRanks

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Show the calendar-year hit rate, the worst single year, and how percentile-rank trends moved year-over-year against the S&P 500 / tracked index. Frame whether the fund's standing is improving, holding, or deteriorating — a 5-year migration up or down the percentile ladder matters more than any single year.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector and country returns swing harder than the broad market — quote the worst single year (sectors regularly lose 30-50% in their bad year) and whether that lined up with broad-market bad years or was a sector-specific drawdown. Quote the same-period S&P 500 calendar-year pattern for contrast.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Returns consistency for leveraged / inverse funds is structurally poor — quote calendar-year wins vs losses, the worst single year (-50% to -90% is common in adverse years), and recovery duration. Retail needs to see in plain numbers that consistency is not a design feature of these products.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Commodities and crypto have extreme calendar-year dispersion — quote the year-by-year pattern (gold: a few +25-30% years amid multi-year flat stretches; BTC: routinely ±50-100% in a single year). Compare hit rate vs S&P 500 over the same window so retail sees the trade-off.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Show how the yield + capped upside translated into total return across each calendar year, including down years where option premium did NOT offset the underlying loss. Quote the worst calendar year against both the equity benchmark and a high-dividend-equity ETF so retail can judge whether the income tilt actually paid off.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Smooth-ride delivery is the core mandate — quote the calendar-year hit rate, the worst single year, and the same-period 100% S&P 500 worst year side-by-side (a moderate-allocation fund should have a materially smaller worst year than pure equity). For target-date glide paths, also compare to the issuer's own glide-path target.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Quote calendar-year hit rate and the worst calendar year (2022 was the rate-shock test — long IG / government lost 25-30%, intermediate core ~13%, ultrashort / MMF ~+1%). Compare worst-year against a duration-matched Treasury index so retail sees whether the loss was rate-cycle inevitable or fund-specific drag.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Quote calendar-year hit rate and the worst single year (2020 COVID drew down HY -15-20%, bank loans -5-10%, EM debt -15%; 2022 hit longer-duration credit harder). Compare worst-year to a matching credit index so retail can see whether the loss was credit-cycle wide or fund-specific.

factors.factorDescription: Separates income contribution from price return. For bond, muni, and income-focused funds, most of the total return comes from distributions rather than price appreciation. Compares total return (return fields) against price-only change (change fields); the gap is the income contribution. A positive total return alongside a declining share price means income is offsetting price erosion — normal for the fund type, not a red flag.
factors.factorMetrics: return1y, change1y, return5y, change5y, return10y, change10y, dividendYield, overviewSecYield, overviewTtmYield, divYears

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Income contribution is only a headline number for explicit dividend / equity-income variants (High Dividend Yield, Large Value with high payout, Dividend Aristocrats-style). For total-market / Large Blend / Large Growth funds the dividend share of total return is small — note it briefly and move on; do not stretch this factor into a yield narrative for a growth-tilted fund.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Unpack the distribution composition — option-premium income, ordinary dividends, short-term capital gains, return of capital. Headline yield without that breakdown is misleading: ROC inflates the yield in good years and erodes NAV; STCG hits at ordinary tax rates. Show total return = distribution yield + NAV change so retail sees the real net result.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Allocation funds pay distributions primarily from the bond sleeve — show how the total return splits between distribution and NAV change. The reader's question is whether the headline yield is funded by real coupon income or whether it includes return-of-capital that quietly drains the fund.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Total return = distribution yield + NAV change. For muni funds, also show tax-equivalent yield (TEY = SEC yield ÷ (1 − marginal tax rate)) at a 32% federal baseline plus state exemption for in-state holders; flag AMT exposure if private-activity bonds are present. Declining NAV alongside a steady distribution is normal in rising-rate environments — not a fund failure.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Total return = distribution yield + NAV change. In stress years (2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock) the distribution kept paying while NAV took hits — retail needs to see both. For high-yield muni, show TEY but flag that the yield premium is paid for taking real default risk, not just the tax shield.

factors.factorDescription: For daily-leveraged or inverse funds, checks whether the realized multi-period return actually matched the daily-leverage target (e.g. 3x, -2x) or whether path-dependency and compounding destroyed the multiple. The long-term return of a leveraged fund is almost never the leverage factor times the underlying's long-term return — the gap is the decay story that must be explained to readers. Flag leveraged / inverse products as short-term trading vehicles only.
factors.factorMetrics: return1m, return3m, return1y, return3y, returnsAnnual, beta, beta1y

groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Test the daily-leverage promise: realized 1Y / 3Y / 5Y CAGR almost never equals the stated multiple times the underlying's CAGR — quote both numbers side-by-side and the gap as compounding-decay loss. State explicitly: these are short-term trading vehicles, never buy-and-hold; the 'how much would $10k be today' question is the wrong frame here.

factors.factorDescription: Reads current price against moving averages (MA20, MA50, MA150, MA200) and RSI levels (daily, weekly, monthly) to judge whether the fund is in an uptrend, downtrend, or sideways range, and whether momentum looks stretched. Why this factor stays despite generally being weak for buy-and-hold equity: it is only assigned to the three groups where entry timing is genuinely decision-useful for retail — (1) sector / thematic / single-country equity, where sector and country cycles are the main driver of forward returns and the buy decision is conviction-plus-timing rather than pure long-term beta; (2) leveraged / inverse products, whose entire suitability case is short-term directional trading and where the trend read IS the analysis; (3) commodities and digital assets, which are momentum-driven asset classes for retail entry — gold breakouts, BTC cycle position, oil supply shocks all show up first in technicals. For broad-equity / allocation / bond / derivative-income groups this factor is deliberately not mapped, because MA / RSI signals are noise relative to the actual drivers (rates, spreads, option premia, glide-path).
factors.factorMetrics: stockPrice, ma20, ma50, ma150, ma200, ma50ChgPercent, ma200ChgPercent, rsi, rsiW, rsiM, high52wChg, low52wChg, athChgPercent, atlChgPercent

groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector cycles are the main driver of forward returns in this group — position vs MA50 / MA200 and RSI levels are decision-useful for retail timing. Quote where price sits in the 52-week range and vs ATH / ATL, flag overbought (monthly RSI >70) or oversold (<30), and tie the technicals back to the sector's macro cycle (rate-sensitive sectors near a rate pivot, energy near supply news, etc.).
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Suitability case is short-term directional trading — the trend position is the entry-timing read, not a long-horizon predictor. Quote price vs MA20 / 50 / 150 / 200 and RSI levels; flag trend direction and stretch (monthly RSI >75 = stretched even by trading standards) so retail sees whether the current entry looks reasonable.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Commodities and digital assets are largely momentum-driven asset classes for retail entry timing — position vs MAs and RSI levels are genuinely decision-useful here in a way they are not for buy-and-hold equity. Quote price vs MA50 / MA200 and RSI levels (monthly RSI >70 = stretched; <30 = washout), and frame current position vs ATH / 52-week range.

factors.factorDescription: Asks the honest retail question: did the fund's actual return profile deliver what its strategy promises? Style funds claim a factor premium (large-value should outperform large-blend over multi-year windows when value is in favor; min-vol should deliver smoother returns than the broad market with comparable long-run CAGR); buffer funds claim a stated cap / floor (the realized return should land between them); long-short and market-neutral claim equity-like or sub-equity return with materially lower drawdown (Sharpe and beta-to-equity should confirm); managed-futures claim crisis alpha (positive returns in equity drawdown years like 2008, 2020, 2022); allocation funds claim a return profile in their stated risk band. Flag the gap when the marketing promise and the realized return story diverge — this is the factor that catches funds where the tilt was cosmetic and the realized return tracked the broad market.
factors.factorMetrics: return1y, return3y, return5y, return10y, returnsAnnual, beta, beta1y, beta2y, beta5y, sharpe, sortino, riskScore, riskLevel

groupInstructions.broad-equity: For style-tilted funds (value, growth, dividend, low-vol, momentum, quality, equal-weight), check that the realized factor premium actually showed up over multi-year windows — large-value should beat large-blend during value cycles; low-vol should show materially lower vol than the S&P with comparable long-run CAGR. For plain-vanilla market-cap-weighted blends (S&P 500, Total Market), mandate alignment collapses to tracking error vs the parent index — keep this section short.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Did the thematic thesis actually deliver in the live history of the fund? Robotics / AI funds should track tech-style returns with thematic-tilted volatility; clean-energy funds should reflect renewables-policy cycles, not just the broad market; infrastructure funds should reflect rate-sensitive yield-plus-growth behavior. Flag funds where the theme was a strong marketing narrative but the realized return tracked the broad market — that means the tilt was cosmetic.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Gold should have negative-to-low correlation with equities and act as a portfolio hedge in equity drawdown windows (2008, 2020 partial, 2022 partial); broad-commodity funds should reflect real-asset / inflation behavior; BTC should track its 'digital gold' or 'growth asset' claim depending on the fund's marketing. Flag funds that pitched a diversification benefit but delivered equity-like correlation in the latest stress window.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Covered-call funds (JEPI, QYLD, SPYI) promise yield + capped upside + cushion in down markets — verify all three actually showed up. Long-short and market-neutral funds promise equity-like or sub-equity return with materially lower drawdown — verify Sharpe and beta-to-equity (target beta below 0.3 for true market-neutral). Managed-futures funds promise crisis alpha — check 2008 / 2020 / 2022 calendar-year returns specifically; if they did not help in those windows the mandate is not being delivered.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Moderate allocation funds should deliver roughly 5-7% long-run CAGR with materially lower drawdown than 100% equity; conservative ~4-5%; aggressive ~7-9%. Target-date funds should track the issuer's published glide path and stay within stated equity-bond-cash bands for the dated year. Tactical and global allocation are evaluated on whether active calls added return — compare to a passive 60/40 same-period CAGR; if not, the mandate is not being delivered.

factors.factorDescription: Tracks the trajectory of distributions over time — has the headline yield held up, ratcheted down, or been propped up by return-of-capital? For income-focused funds, retail buyers expect today's distribution to keep paying tomorrow, but many specialty-yield products quietly cut as underlying spreads compress, rate cuts roll through reset coupons, or option premiums shrink in low-vol regimes. Quote per-share distribution per year over the available history, flag any reductions, and break out ROC share of distributions in the latest year where it is structurally material. For bond funds, distribution should track SEC yield closely; large gaps signal smoothing or accounting yield that will not last.
factors.factorMetrics: dividendYield, overviewSecYield, overviewTtmYield, dividendTtm, divGrowth3y, divGrowth5y, divYears, divGrYears, payoutFrequency

groupInstructions.derivative-income: Headline yields on covered-call funds (JEPI 7-10%, QYLD 10-12%, SPYI 12%+) are option-premium driven and swing with implied volatility — in low-vol regimes the premium shrinks and the distribution often follows. Quote per-share distribution year-by-year, the latest-year ROC share (often material for these funds), and flag whether the headline yield is being preserved by NAV erosion.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Distribution should closely track SEC yield. For Treasury and IG corporate funds, distribution naturally rose in 2022-2024 as rates climbed and eased again as rates fell — that is coupon-cycle behavior, not fund quality. Flag funds where distribution diverged materially from SEC yield (signal of smoothing or de-accumulated coupons).
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: HY and bank-loan distributions move with reference rates (SOFR-linked coupons reset quarterly) and with default cycles (yield can rise short-term as prices fall in stress, then distribution gets cut as defaulted positions stop paying). Quote per-share distribution year-by-year, especially around 2020 and 2022, and break out the ROC share for preferreds and converts where it is structurally larger.
